forecasts.reasoning: As of May 1, 2025, Kilmar Abrego Garcia remains in a Salvadoran maximum-security prison (CECOT). While the U.S. Supreme Court and a federal district judge have ordered the Trump administration to facilitate his return to the U.S., the administration has shown extreme defiance. On May 1, 2025, White House spokesperson Tricia McLaughlin explicitly stated, 'Kilmar Abrego Garcia will never be on American streets again,' and the administration has filed documents indicating potential plans to divert him to a third country (like Uganda) rather than return him to the U.S. Given the physical detention in El Salvador (whose government also opposes his return) and the explicit refusal of the U.S. executive branch to comply with court orders before the July 1 deadline, the probability of him entering U.S. territory in the next 60 days is very low. Physical return requires executive cooperation which is currently being withheld.

Multiple reputable defense news sources and the official South Korean news agency (Yonhap) reported in January 2025 that South Korea and Vietnam were in the 'final stages' of a $276-300 million deal for 20 K9 self-propelled howitzers. Subsequent reporting in March 2025 from outlets like Army Recognition and Overt Defense explicitly stated that Vietnam had 'selected' the K9 and that a contract had been 'signed' or 'unveiled.' Given that negotiations were in their final stages as of January and confirmed as a selection/purchase by March, the official government-to-government announcement requirement is highly likely to be met before the July 1, 2025 deadline. The specificity of the transaction details (20 units, $276-300m, K9A1 variant) across multiple sources strongly indicates an agreement has been reached.

The Carnegie Global Protest Tracker has been updated as of April 2025 with data reflecting the ongoing political crisis in South Korea. Multiple massive protests occurred in early 2025, well after the likely launch date of this question (informed by a December 2024 ACLED report). Specific attendance figures for 2025 include 38,000 in February, 110,000 in March, and over 20,000 in April (search_1_result_4, search_1_result_5, search_1_result_0). These figures easily exceed the 10,000-participant threshold. A secondary source (Democracy Without Borders) confirms the tracker was updated with recent South Korean data as of April 7, 2025. Given the scale of the South Korean protest movement following President Yoon's martial law declaration, it is extremely likely that the tracker records at least one 10,000+ participant event for this period.

While North Korea has maintained technical readiness for a seventh nuclear test since 2022, and March 2025 US intelligence reports specifically warned of readiness 'on short notice,' the actual likelihood in a narrow two-month window (May-June 2025) remains low. Major holidays in April 2025 passed without a test, and recent North Korean military activity and rhetoric have focused on diversifying delivery systems (naval and missiles) rather than an imminent underground detonation. Given the 7.5-year hiatus since the last test and the lack of specific imminent indicators in April 2025, the probability is set at 8%.

As of May 1, 2025, the United States has not accused Russia of invading any country other than Ukraine. Evidence from recent search results indicates a significant shift in U.S. foreign policy following the inauguration of a new administration in January 2025. In February 2025, the U.S. moved to a 'neutral' stance at the United Nations, even introducing a resolution regarding the Ukraine war that notably omitted any mention of Russia's aggression or calls for its withdrawal (search_5_result_8). This policy of de-escalation and neutrality makes it highly improbable that the U.S. government would issue a new accusation of invasion regarding Russia's presence in other countries (such as Moldova or Georgia) before the July 1 deadline. Furthermore, there have been no credible reports of new Russian ground force incursions into other territories without consent during the 2024-2025 period. The probability reflects the extremely low likelihood of such a diplomatic reversal or a new military event occurring in the remaining two-month window.

As of early 2025, 48 African Union member states have deposited their instruments of ratification for the AfCFTA. Libya has been consistently listed among the seven holdouts (Benin, Liberia, Libya, Madagascar, Somalia, South Sudan, and Sudan) that have signed but not yet ratified the agreement. While the Libyan Ministry of Economy and Trade has engaged in capacity-building workshops and submitted provisional tariff schedules, the country remains mired in a political stalemate between the Government of National Unity (GNU) and the House of Representatives (HoR), which significantly hinders the formal legislative process required for treaty ratification. With no evidence of any legislative breakthrough as of May 1, 2025, it is highly unlikely that Libya will complete the ratification and deposition process by the July 1, 2025 deadline.
